summary: Gruber provides a detailed tutorial on adding PHP syntax checking to BBEdit using PHP's command-line tool and AppleScript. He explains how PHP's -l flag performs rudimentary parse-error detection, then walks through building an AppleScript that handles unsaved files via temporary copies, displays errors in alerts with line-number navigation, and integrates with BBEdit's existing HTML syntax checker through menu script attachability. The script uses dual handlers so it works both as a standalone Scripts menu item and as a supplement to BBEdit's built-in Check Document Syntax command.
key_points: PHP's -l flag only catches parse errors and stops at the first one found, making it rudimentary but still useful
The script handles edge cases like unsaved changes, untitled documents, and non-Unix line endings by writing temporary files
BBEdit's menu script attachability allows combining PHP syntax checking with the built-in HTML checker through a single script with dual handlers
The script extracts line numbers from PHP error messages to select the offending line in the editor
money_quotes: I say "rudimentary" because, as far as I can tell, PHP's syntax checker only detects parse errors — errors so egregious they cause PHP to be unable to parse the code.
Still, it's better than no checker at all.
However, step 1, as stated above, is a bit lazy. What if the front BBEdit window has unsaved changes?
We could have the script save the file automatically if it has unsaved changes. But that's still lazy — we should be able to syntax-check a file with unsaved changes.
Thus, if BBEdit displays its "No errors were found" dialog, we know that the file passed through both syntax checkers without complaint.
key_insight: BBEdit's extensibility through AppleScript and menu script attachability enables developers to build sophisticated tool integrations that handle real-world edge cases like unsaved files and combined syntax checking.
